units.title: The Architects of Matter
units.competency: Discuss the significant contributions of early scientists in the development of the periodic table of elements.

questions.prompt: What does the presence of these "blanks" in Mendeleev's model reveal about the fundamental difference between his scientific reasoning and that of Newlands?
options: Newlands believed the "keyboard" was already complete, while Mendeleev realized several "notes" (elements) had not yet been discovered.
Mendeleev organized elements by musical frequency, whereas Newlands focused only on the physical weight of the keys.
Newlands ignored the transition metals entirely, while Mendeleev placed them in the "blanks" to maintain a perfect octave.
Mendeleev’s blanks proved that atomic mass was an unreliable metric, leading him to switch to atomic numbers immediately.

questions.prompt:
In the "Dobereiner’s Triads" section of the exhibit, there is a display case with three mineral samples found in Benguet. The label for the middle sample is missing its mass.
Sample 1 (Calcium): Mass = 40
Sample 2 (Strontium): Mass = ?
Sample 3 (Barium): Mass = 137
According to "Dobereiner’s   rule, the mass of the middle element (Sample 2) should be the average of Sample 1 and Sample 3.
Calculate the mass of Sample 2 (Strontium).

A group of students is confused about the difference between Mendeleev (The Fortune Teller) and Moseley (The Modern Fixer). Help them by checking the correct box for each statement.
Put a check (✔) in the correct column.

Mendeleev left gaps (marked with '?') in his periodic table. Why did he do this, and how did he justify leaving these spots empty instead of just grouping the known elements together?
A museum visitor asks you: "Why is this table famous? It looks incomplete. Did he just forget to finish it?"
Using the information in the Cheat Sheet, write a 1-2 sentence response to the visitor explaining why the blank spaces were a brilliant scientific contribution.

stimulusBlocks.label: Situation
stimulusBlocks.title: The Architects of Matter
stimulusBlocks.content: You are a Junior Curator (an intern) at the National Museum of the Philippines in Manila. The Head Curator, Ms. Reyes, is setting up a new exhibit called "The Architects of Matter”.This exhibit tells the story of how early scientists struggled to organize the chemical elements before the modern Periodic Table was created. Ms. Reyes hands you a "Cheat Sheet" summarizing the four main scientists in the exhibit. She needs you to use this guide to verify the display labels.

stimulusBlocks.label: Table
stimulusBlocks.title: Ms. Reyes' Cheat Sheet
rows: The Triads: He grouped elements into threes.He noticed that the atomic mass of the middle element is approximately the  average of the other two.

Law of Octaves:   Newland’s musical analogy illustrates the repeating patterns of elements, but it's a simplification. While the first few rows show a repeating pattern every eight elements, the discovery of heavier elements later revealed that the 'length' of these periods actually changes.
The Fortune Teller: He arranged elements by mass but was the first to leave blank spaces in the table. He predicted that undiscovered elements belonged in those spaces.
The Modern Fixer: He discovered that arranging elements by Atomic Number (number of protons) was better than using mass. This fixed the errors in the previous tables.
stimulusBlocks.note: Newlands tried to force every element into a strict musical pattern, even when they didn't fit. Mendeleev was smarter: he realized that if the "music" didn't sound right, it wasn't because the pattern was wrong—it was because some "notes" (elements) were still missing. > By leaving blanks, Mendeleev proved he understood that science is an unfinished puzzle, whereas Newlands tried to finish the puzzle with missing pieces

units.title: The Atom Builder
units.competency:
1. Draw the structure of an atom in terms of the nucleus and electron shells;                                           
2. Differentiate the subatomic particles protons, neutrons, and electrons in terms of their symbol, mass, charge, and location within an atom.

questions.prompt:
Marra selects a particle with the symbol. She tries to drop it into the Outer Rings, but the game displays an error message: "Invalid Location for this Mass."
Based on the table, where must Marra place the particle, and which particle should she have selected if she wanted to place something in the Outer Rings?

Placement for : 
1.___________

Correct particle for Rings:
2. ___________

Marra is building a Lithium atom. She has already placed 3 Protons in the center. The "Charge Meter" currently reads.
According to the Particle Inventory, what must Marra add to the "Outer Rings" to make the atom's total charge Neutral (0), and how many are required?
 Explain your reasoning.
questions.sampleAnswer: Mara add 3 Electrons to the outer ring to make the atom neutral. Since there are already 3 protons and protons are positively  charged and electrons are negative, so +3 + -3 = 0. That makes the atom neutral.

questions.prompt:
After finishing her atom, Marra looks at the Total Mass Display. She has used a total of 10 particles (3 Protons, 4 Neutrons, and 3 Electrons). However, the game shows the "Atomic Mass" as only 7 amu.
Using the "Relative Mass" column in the table, explain why the 3 electrons contribute ~0 amu; only p and n count toward atomic mass?
questions.sampleAnswer: The mass is 7 because electrons are are almost negligible or too light to matter compared to the heavy protons and neutrons

questions.prompt:
The game allows a "Sandbox Mode" where Marra can create a custom particle. She creates a particle with a Mass of 1 amu and a Charge of -1.

Why would this particle be considered not part of the standard set  in this model(proton , neutron, electron)
questions.sampleAnswer: Mass-1 particles stay in the nucleus and are not negative and that -1 charge particles are always negligible in mass.

stimulusBlocks.label: Situation
stimulusBlocks.title: The Atom Builder
stimulusBlocks.content:
Marra is using a digital laboratory tool called "Atom Builder." In this game, she must construct stable atoms by dragging particles into the correct zones. The interface consists of two distinct areas:
The Nucleus (Center Zone): A small, dense cluster in the middle.
The Electron Cloud (Outer Rings): Transparent paths surrounding the center.

units.title: The Glow of Manila
units.competency: Differentiate the subatomic particles protons, neutrons, and electrons in terms of their symbol, mass, charge, and location within an atom.

questions.prompt: Marco is analyzing why Neon and Argon are in the same column (Group 18) but different rows (Periods). Indicate "Yes" or "No" for each statement regarding their electron structure and position.
questions.sampleAnswer:
1. Yes (Both have full valence shells—stable octet).
2. No (Neon has 2 shells; Argon has 3).
3. Yes (Group 18 elements have full valence shells/8 valence electrons, except Helium).
4. No (Period number corresponds to the number of shells, not protons).

questions.prompt:
Look at the electron shell diagram Marco drew. Neon is in Period 2, while Argon is in Period 3 of the Periodic Table.
Explain how the electron structure determines which Period (row) an element belongs to. Use the diagrams of Neon and Argon to support your answer.
questions.sampleAnswer: Period number equals the number of occupied electron shells (energy levels). Explicitly mentions that Neon has 2 shells (Period 2) and Argon has 3 shells (Period 3).

questions.prompt:
A new restaurant in Makati wants a sign with a different color. They are considering using Krypton (Kr), which is in Period 4, Group 18.
Without seeing a diagram of Krypton, which statement correctly describes its electron structure based on its position in the Periodic Table?
options: It has 4 valence electrons and 18 shells
It has 8 valence electrons and 4 shells.
It has 18 valence electrons and 4 shells.
It has 4 valence electrons and 4 shells.

questions.prompt:
While buying materials at the hardware store, the shopkeeper warns Ligaya: "Make sure the glass tubes are sealed tight! These gases are safe, but don't try to use something like Sodium vapor without expert help.“Ligaya knows that Sodium (Na) is in Group 1 and is very reactive (unstable), while Neon and Argon (Group 18) are unreactive (stable) "Noble Gases."
Sodium has 1 valence electron. Neon/Argon have 8 valence electrons.
Explain why having 8 valence electrons makes the Noble gases suitable for a safe parol light, while having 1 valence electron makes Sodium too reactive
questions.sampleAnswer: Atoms are most stable when they have a full outer shell of 8 electrons, like the Noble gases. This is why they don't react. Sodium is unstable because it has only 1 outer electron, it wants to give away to become stable, making it very reactive.

stimulusBlocks.label: Situation
stimulusBlocks.title: The Glow of Manila
stimulusBlocks.content:
If you walk along the busy streets of Metro Manila at night, you are surrounded by a city that never truly goes dark. From the towering, high-definition billboards lining EDSA to the vibrant "Open" signs of small sari-sari stores in Quiapo, colorful light defines the landscape.
While locals often refer to all these glowing displays as "neon," a physics student from the University of the Philippines named Marco knows there is a hidden scientific distinction. As he observes the towering screens on EDSA, he realizes they aren’t neon at all; they are massive arrays of LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes) using solid-state semiconductors. However, tucked away in the older alleys of Quiapo or the trendy bars of Makati, he finds what he is looking for: genuine glass neon tubes. He notices the difference immediately—where LED signs are bright and pixelated, real neon signs emit a continuous, organic hum, acting as a literal glowing bridge to the Periodic Table.
Marco’s research explains that the specific color of light emitted depends on the unique electron structure of the gas trapped inside the glass tube. The process follows a specific cycle:
Excitation: As electricity flows through the tube, it strikes the gas atoms, bumping their electrons to a higher energy level.
Relaxation: These electrons are unstable at higher levels and quickly return to their ground state.
Emission: As they return, they release their excess energy as photons (light).

A Comparison of Noble Neighbors
Marco compares two "Noble" cousins from Group 18 often used in these signs. Because these elements have full valence shells, they are stable enough to survive the constant electrical "pumping" without reacting.

stimulusBlocks.label: Visual
stimulusBlocks.title: Visual Comparison
stimulusBlocks.note: Marco sketches a diagram of their electron shells, noting that while both provide stunning colors, their different atomic sizes and structures result in the distinct "fingerprints" of light that color the Manila night.

units.title: The Salt of Pangasinan
units.competency:
Describe the properties of pure substances as:
a. having fixed chemical composition, examples of which are elements and compounds, and                                
b. that all the atoms of an element have a unique number of protons;   
Calculate the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons in the atom of several elements, such as aluminum.

questions.prompt:
Based on the descriptions provided, categorize the materials found in a salt farm.
Check ( ✔) one bos in eachh below.

Using the information in the table, justify why the composition of seawater can vary from one location to another, while the composition of Table Salt (NaCl) always remains the same. Write your explanation below by comparing the chemical nature of seawater and table salt.
questions.sampleAnswer: Seawater is a mixture (or solution) and table salt is a compound. The mixtures do not have a fixed ratio and can contain different amounts of substances (salt, algae, sand), whereas a compound like NaCl is chemically .Sodium and Chlorine ,bonded in a fixed 1:1 ratio.

questions.prompt:
Sodium (Na)- Atomic Number 11) and Magnesium (Mg) -Atomic Number 12) are both found in seawater, but they have different properties.
Based on the rule that all atoms of an element have a unique number of protons, indicate whether the following statements are True or False. Put  ( ✔) on the corresponding column.

In a science fiction movie, a machine "zaps" a block of Pure Sodium (Na, Atomic Number 11) and turns it into Magnesium (Mg, Atomic Number 12) instantly.
Scientifically, what exactly must the machine do to the nucleus of the Sodium atoms to make this change possible?
 Explain why a simple chemical reaction cannot achieve this
questions.sampleAnswer: The machine would have to add exactly one proton to the nucleus of every Sodium atom. Chemical reactions cannot do this because they only share or transfer electrons; they never touch the protons in the nucleus.

stimulusBlocks.label: Situation
stimulusBlocks.title: The Salt of Pangasinan
stimulusBlocks.content:
The province of Pangasinan is named after "asin" (salt). In towns like Bolinao and Dasol, salt farmers, evaporate seawater under the hot sun to produce salt.
The main component of this salt is sodium chloride (NaCl). However, health officials often recommend adding Iodine (I) to the salt to prevent thyroid problems by creating "Iodized Salt."

stimulusBlocks.label: Table
stimulusBlocks.title: Scientific Data
rows: A soft, silvery metal. It is highly reactive and has an atomic number of 11.
A white crystal formed by 1 Sodium atom bonding with 1 Chlorine
A liquid solution of water, dissolved salts , sand and algae
